title: [epic] Project utils system: typed utility scripts, manifest, and process manager
description:
## Overview

Introduce a first-class `utils/` system to xtrm-tools — a set of typed, manifested utility scripts that specialists and orchestrators can invoke declaratively, plus an abstract process manager module for long-lived/background processes.

## Architecture

Xtreme is the execution substrate (worktrees, process lifecycle, toolchain, hooks). This epic formalizes that role with concrete tooling.

### Layer boundary (to document formally)

- **Xtreme** = provisioning, worktrees, process lifecycle, session management, toolchain, shared scripts, pre/post hooks
- **Specialist** = role logic, task execution, output, handoff — consumes Xtreme primitives

## Planned sub-epics / bundles

### 1. Utils folder + typed manifest (foundation)
- `utils/manifest.json` schema: id, description, runtime, command, input, output, timeout, phase (pre/post/on-demand), failure_policy (blocking/advisory)
- Every utility declares its contract; no ad-hoc scripts

### 2. Utility script bundles (implement manifest contract)
- **Core** — health-check, lint, type-check, format-check, test-smoke
- **Validators** — schema-validator (JSON/YAML/specialist-output), config-validator (env, project config), policy-validator (naming, structure, ownership)
- **Security** — secret-scan, dependency-vulnerability-check, unsafe-pattern-scan
- **Git/change intelligence** — changed-files-analyzer (classifies by type), diff-risk-estimator (coupling + missing tests), branch-consistency-check
- **Testing** — targeted-test-selector (runs tests covering changed files only), coverage-delta-check, flaky-test-detector
- **Observability** — log-follow-helper, error-summarizer, incident-snapshot (processes + ports + logs + repo state bundle)
- **Docker** — docker-health, docker-compose-validate, image-policy-check

### 3. Specialist integration
- Expose utils manifest to specialist YAML via `capabilities.utils[]`
- Specialists declare which utils to run at which phase (pre/in/post)

### 4. Abstract process manager module
- Interface: start / stop / restart / status / logs / follow / cleanup
- PNPM-backed as first implementation; interface-abstracted for future runtimes
- Named processes, PID + log tracking, follow streaming, reattach to existing
- Process policies: timeout, restart policy, blocking vs background classification
- Automatic cleanup at job end
- Enables specialists to launch dev servers, log tails, event streams, watchers — and human/agent operators to follow with `--follow`

## Sequencing

1. Architecture doc (formal Xtreme vs Specialist boundary)
2. Utils manifest schema
3. Core + Validator bundles (highest utility for specialists)
4. Process manager (unlocks background process UX)
5. Remaining bundles + specialist integration

## Notes

This epic is intentionally coarse-grained. Each sub-item above becomes its own issue when the time comes to implement it. File this as a planning anchor — break it down before starting.

Upstream ask or local Serena wrapper.

It should also detect if a .serena/ config exists in the project cwd, record the project name in .beads/.last_active bundle, and inject an additionalSystemPrompt on restore like 'Serena was active on project X — re-activate with activate_project before any symbol lookups.' This is a pure shell change: check for .serena/config.yml or .serena/ dir in save hook, add serenaProject field to the JSON bundle, emit reminder in restore hook.

title: Suppress duplicate xtrm-ui extension conflict noise in Pi
description: When running Pi from within the xtrm-tools project, Pi auto-discovers config/pi/extensions/xtrm-ui/index.ts via the 'pi' key in its package.json, AND also loads the globally installed ~/.pi/agent/extensions/xtrm-ui/. This causes noisy conflict warnings at startup (tool/command conflicts). The project-local version wins so behavior is correct, but the warnings are ugly. Options: (1) remove the global copy since project-local always wins in this repo, (2) find a way to suppress auto-discovery for the project-local source copy, or (3) check if Pi 0.63.1 has a way to exclude paths from auto-discovery.

title: Derive canonical lists dynamically in clean command
description:
In clean.ts, canonical hooks and skills are hardcoded:

```typescript
const CANONICAL_HOOKS = new Set([...]);
const CANONICAL_SKILLS = new Set([...]);
```

These lists must be manually kept in sync with actual repo contents. If a new skill is added but forgotten in this list, xtrm clean will delete it.

**Impact**: Data loss if new skills/hooks are added without updating the hardcoded lists.

**Recommendation**: Derive canonical lists dynamically from repo contents (scan hooks/ and skills/ directories).

title: Feature: Remove Turn counter from statusline
description:
## Problem

The Turn counter in the statusline () provides no actionable value. Users don't need to track conversation turn count — it's internal state that doesn't inform decision-making.

## Current Display

```
XTRM [Turn 3] | claude-sonnet | 12% | ⌂ xtrm-tools | ⎇ feature/foo
```

## Proposed Display

```
XTRM | claude-sonnet | 12% | ⌂ xtrm-tools | ⎇ feature/foo
```

## Changes

### File: `config/pi/extensions/custom-footer.ts`

Remove:
1. `let turnCount = 0;` variable
2. `pi.on("turn_start", ...)` handler
3. `pi.on("session_switch", ...)` handler
4. `const turns = theme.fg("dim", "[Turn ${turnCount}]");` in render
5. Reference to `turns` in the output string

### Before

```typescript
let turnCount = 0;

pi.on("turn_start", async () => {
  turnCount++;
});

// In render:
const turns = theme.fg("dim", "[Turn ${turnCount}]");

const leftParts = [`${brand} ${turns}`, modelStr, usageStr, cwdStr];
```

### After

```typescript
// In render:
const leftParts = [brand, modelStr, usageStr, cwdStr];
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Turn counter removed from statusline
- [ ] No `turnCount` variable or handlers in custom-footer.ts
- [ ] Layout still balanced without the Turn element

## Related

- Issue: xtrm-6g0 (beads statusline integration — will add chip in same area)
close_reason: Implemented in 1ed9c54 (feat/statusline beads bold #71)

title: Eval: consolidate beads hook files vs extract shared utils
description:
## Context

This project is **xtrm-tools** — a collection of Claude Code agent skills, hooks, and tooling for a multi-agent development workflow. Hooks are shell scripts (mostly .mjs) that fire at lifecycle events in Claude Code sessions (PreToolUse, PostToolUse, Stop, etc.) and enforce workflow rules.

## The Hooks in Question

Four beads hooks live in `~/.claude/hooks/` (installed globally, not tracked in this repo):

| File | Event | Purpose |
|------|-------|---------|
| `beads-edit-gate.mjs` | PreToolUse (Write/Edit) | Blocks file edits if this session has no claimed issue |
| `beads-stop-gate.mjs` | Stop | Blocks agent stop if this session has an unclaimed issue |
| `beads-commit-gate.mjs` | PreToolUse (Bash/git commit) | Blocks git commit if this session has an unclaimed issue |
| `beads-close-memory-prompt.mjs` | PostToolUse (Bash/bd close) | Clears kv claim + injects reminder after bd close |

## How They Work (Recent Refactor)

All three gate hooks now use **session-scoped claiming via `bd kv`** instead of global in_progress checks:

```js
// Shared pattern in all three gate hooks (currently duplicated):
function getSessionClaim(sessionId) {
  try {
    return execSync(`bd kv get "claimed:${sessionId}"`, { ... }).trim();
  } catch (err) {
    if (err.status === 1) return '';  // key not found
    return null;                      // bd kv unavailable — fail open
  }
}
```

Each hook also duplicates: exec options boilerplate, `.beads` dir check, cwd resolution, and the fallback path (no session_id → old global in_progress check).

## The Question for Evaluation

Two options were proposed:

### Option A — Single dispatcher script
One `beads-gate.mjs` file handles all events, routes internally by inspecting `input.hook_type` or similar.

**Key constraint**: Claude Code does NOT pass the hook event type in the hook input JSON. So `settings.json` still needs separate entries per event — each pointing to the same file. The file would need another mechanism to know which path to take (e.g. a CLI flag, or env var set per-entry in settings.json, e.g. `BEADS_HOOK_MODE=edit node beads-gate.mjs`).

### Option B — Shared utils module
Keep four separate hook files. Extract duplicated logic into `beads-gate-utils.mjs`:
- `getSessionClaim(sessionId, cwd)`
- `execBd(cmd, cwd)`
- `hasTotalWork(cwd)`
- `checkBeadsProject(cwd)`

Each hook imports from utils. Files stay small and independently testable.

## What We Want You to Evaluate

1. Is Option A actually viable given the Claude Code hook input constraint (no event type in JSON)? Is there a clean way to pass routing context per-entry in settings.json?
2. Is Option B the pragmatic choice, or does it add complexity for modest gain?
3. Is there an Option C we haven't considered?
4. What's the blast radius / maintenance risk of each approach over time, as more hooks get added?

Please post your recommendation as a comment or note on this issue. The author will implement based on your answer.
close_reason: Implemented: shared utils extracted, all hooks updated, withSafeBdContext added

comments.author: you@example.com
comments.text:
Recommendation: choose Option B now (separate hook entrypoints + shared utils module), with a narrow Option C fallback if/when complexity grows.

1) Option A viability
- Technically viable only if you pass explicit mode per settings entry (CLI arg or env var), since hook JSON does not include event type.
- That means you still keep multiple settings entries, and now add a dispatch contract that can drift (mode string mismatch, missing arg/env).
- Net: centralization gain is smaller than it appears, while routing fragility increases.

2) Option B pragmatism
- Best current tradeoff. Keep event-specific scripts for clarity and independent behavior, extract only duplicated primitives:
  - resolveCwd/input parsing
  - checkBeadsProject
  - execBd/getSessionClaim
  - fallback global in_progress path
- This removes repetition without coupling unrelated policy decisions into one dispatcher.

3) Option C (recommended guardrail)
- Hybrid: Option B + one tiny shared guard wrapper for uniform error/timeout/fail-open policy (e.g., withSafeBdContext(fn)).
- Keep policy logic in each hook, infra logic in utils. Avoid full dispatcher until >~8 hooks or heavy shared branching emerges.

4) Blast radius / maintenance risk
- Option A: high blast radius. A dispatcher bug or routing regression can break all hook events at once.
- Option B: low-to-medium blast radius. Utils bugs affect many hooks, but event logic stays isolated and easier to test.
- Option C hybrid: medium, but controlled if shared wrapper is very small and stable.

Decision rule going forward
- Stay on Option B while hooks remain mostly independent.
- Reconsider dispatcher only when duplication is primarily in policy branching (not just shell/bd plumbing) and you can enforce strict mode validation + tests per event mode.

title: Investigate: bd create may auto-set in_progress, triggering stop gate
description: After claiming an issue (bd update --status=in_progress + bd kv set), the stop gate showed in_progress on a freshly-created issue before any explicit status change. Possible causes: bd create defaults to in_progress status, or beads-edit-gate's bd kv claiming logic has a side effect on issue state. Reproduce: create a fresh issue, check status immediately, observe if stop gate fires. Compare behavior with and without the new session-scoped kv claiming code.
notes: ROOT CAUSE FOUND: Not bd create defaulting to in_progress (confirmed: new issues are always created as OPEN). The stop gate false-positive is a Dolt read inconsistency: bd list --status=in_progress reads the Dolt *working set* while bd show reads the *committed* state. With dolt.auto-commit=off (default), bd update writes to the working set immediately but bd close may only commit after the fact. Result: bd list sees stale in_progress from the working set even though bd show reports CLOSED from HEAD. The new session-scoped stop gate (kv claim check) sidesteps this entirely — it won't fire for a session with no kv claim, regardless of what bd list shows. Mitigation: ensure bd close always flushes the working set, OR document that dolt.auto-commit=on is required for gate consistency.
close_reason: Diagnosed: Dolt working-set vs committed-state read inconsistency, not bd create behavior

title: Add PreCompact/SessionStart hooks to restore in_progress beads state after compaction
description:
After /compact, beads issue statuses persist in Dolt but the agent resumes without knowing which issues were in_progress. The gate fires on the first write and the agent must manually re-mark issues before continuing.

Fix: ship two hooks as part of xtrm-tools (installed via xtrm install into ~/.claude/hooks/):

1. PreCompact hook (new): hooks/beads-compact-save.py (or .cjs)
   - Runs bd list --status=in_progress
   - Writes issue IDs to .beads/.last_active (one ID per line)
   - Fires before context is compacted so the list is accurate

2. SessionStart hook addition: extend existing hooks or add hooks/beads-compact-restore.cjs
   - On session start, checks for .beads/.last_active
   - If found, runs bd update <id> --status=in_progress for each ID
   - Deletes .beads/.last_active after restore
   - Injects a brief context message: 'Restored N in_progress issues from last session'

Both hooks wired in config/settings.json and synced by xtrm install.

Note: beads-edit-gate.mjs is owned by the beads npm package and should not be modified directly. Our hooks complement it without patching upstream. If the beads package ever ships its own compaction support, our hooks can be retired.

Acceptance criteria:
- /compact followed by new session → first write is NOT blocked by the gate
- Issues that were in_progress before compact are still in_progress after
- .beads/.last_active is cleaned up after restore (idempotent)
close_reason: Closed

title: Register hello command in cli/src/index.ts
description:
## Context

Part of the xtrm-hello epic (xtrm-wdt7). Depends on xtrm-wdt7.1 (the command module must exist first). This is the shell/wiring layer task: import the factory and add it to the Commander program.

## What to build

Edit \`cli/src/index.ts\`:
1. Add import: \`import { createHelloCommand } from './commands/hello.js';\`
2. Add to program: \`program.addCommand(createHelloCommand());\`

Place the import alongside the other command imports (alphabetical order near the help/install block). Place \`addCommand\` call in the same region as the other \`addCommand\` calls.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] \`xtrm hello\` (after build) prints \`Hello\` and exits 0
- [ ] \`xtrm --help\` lists \`hello\` in the commands section
- [ ] Build succeeds: \`cd cli && npm run build\`
- [ ] No lint errors

## Approach notes

- File to edit: \`cli/src/index.ts\`
- Layer: **shell** (wires the factory into the CLI program)
- All other commands follow the same addCommand pattern — just copy the style

title: Implement hello command — cli/src/commands/hello.ts
description:
## Context

Part of the xtrm-hello epic (xtrm-wdt7). This is the core layer task: create the command module that contains the pure output logic. No I/O dependencies beyond stdout; no HTTP, no DB, no filesystem reads.

## What to build

Create \`cli/src/commands/hello.ts\` exporting \`createHelloCommand(): Command\` that:
- Uses Commander.js \`new Command('hello')\`
- Sets description: \`'Say hello and stop'\`
- In the \`.action()\` handler: calls \`console.log('Hello')\` and returns (Commander handles exit)

Follow the pattern from \`cli/src/commands/help.ts\` (same shape: factory function, no external deps).

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] File \`cli/src/commands/hello.ts\` exists and compiles (\`cd cli && npx tsc --noEmit\`)
- [ ] \`createHelloCommand()\` returns a valid Commander Command
- [ ] Running the action prints exactly \`Hello\` to stdout
- [ ] No lint errors (\`npm run lint\` clean)

## Approach notes

- Pattern to follow: \`cli/src/commands/help.ts\` — minimal factory, no imports except Commander
- Naming: \`createHelloCommand\` matches the \`create<Name>Command\` convention used everywhere
- Layer: **core** (pure logic, no I/O side effects beyond console.log)
close_reason: Implemented hello command with Commander.js factory pattern, registered in index.ts, unit + integration tests all passing
